title: How asset classes behave relative to each other
subtitle: Owning different things only helps if those things don't all crash at the same time.

body.content: Owning multiple asset classes only reduces risk if they don't all fall at once. That's the whole point. The mathematical measure of how much two assets move together is correlation — and understanding it changes how you think about building a portfolio. It's not about what you own. It's about how the things you own relate to each other.

Correlation ranges from −1 to +1. A correlation of +1 means two assets move in perfect lockstep — when one goes up 5%, the other goes up 5%. −1 means perfect inverse — one goes up 5%, the other goes down 5%. 0 means no relationship. In real portfolios, correlations are always somewhere in between and they change over time. The useful target: combine assets with correlations closer to 0 or negative, so when one falls, the other doesn't necessarily follow.

body.label: Key concept
body.content: Stocks and long-term US Treasury bonds have historically had a negative or near-zero correlation. When stocks fall sharply (typically during recessions), the Fed often cuts rates, which causes bond prices to rise. This is why the classic 60/40 portfolio (60% stocks, 40% bonds) has worked so well for decades — the two assets partially offset each other. You give up some equity upside in exchange for meaningfully smoother overall returns.
How different pairs of asset classes correlate

items: US stocks vs. US bonds: typically negative or low — the classic diversifier pairing
US stocks vs. international stocks: positive but not perfect — geographic diversification helps
US stocks vs. gold: low/slightly negative — gold sometimes holds up in equity crashes
US stocks vs. real estate (REITs): moderate positive — both respond to economic growth
US stocks vs. commodities: low — commodities often benefit from inflation that hurts stocks

body.content: The problem: correlations that look stable in normal times tend to converge toward 1 during a financial crisis. When liquidity dries up and panic spreads, investors sell everything. 2008 showed this clearly — correlations between almost all risk assets spiked dramatically as the crisis deepened. The diversification benefit you counted on was smaller when you needed it most. This doesn't make diversification useless — it's still far better than concentration — but it should temper expectations about what it can guarantee.

When building a portfolio, don't just ask 'does this asset have good returns?' Ask 'how does this asset behave relative to what I already own?' Adding a second tech stock to a portfolio of tech stocks does almost nothing for diversification — they're highly correlated. Adding bonds, international equities, or REITs to a US equity portfolio adds meaningfully different behavior. The goal is a portfolio where not everything breaks at the same time.

body.label: Real talk
body.content: In 2022, the stock-bond correlation briefly flipped positive — both fell simultaneously as inflation spiked and the Fed aggressively hiked rates. It was the worst year for the 60/40 portfolio in decades. The relationship that had held for 40 years briefly broke down. This is a feature of reality: correlations are not laws. They're patterns. Useful patterns — but not guarantees.

Diversification works through low or negative correlation between assets. Stocks and bonds have historically been the classic inverse pair. In crises, correlations converge — everything falls together. This doesn't invalidate diversification, it just means you can't count on it as a crisis shield. It works over full market cycles, not just the bad days.
